title: Premium Membership Launch
problem_statement: ZaikaNow is exploring a premium membership tier — 'ZaikaNow Gold' — to boost retention and revenue among high-value customers. Analyze the customer base, ordering patterns, and satisfaction signals to design the membership offering.

problems.title: ZaikaNow Gold Membership Design
problems.challenge_prompt: ZaikaNow wants to launch a premium loyalty tier to retain high-value users and grow revenue. Analyze customer segments, ordering patterns, payment behavior, and user satisfaction to design the membership offering and identify the right target audience.
problems.challenge_problem_statement: ZaikaNow, an India-native food delivery marketplace, is considering launching 'ZaikaNow Gold' — a premium membership tier. Using 6 months of customer, order, payment, review, and operational data, segment the customer base, identify the highest-value users, understand their needs and pain points, and design a membership offering with clear target audience, feature set, pricing rationale, and launch strategy.
problems.root_cause: N/A — this is a strategic scenario. The ideal output is a well-segmented membership design backed by data analysis.
key_evidence: Power users (top segment) have significantly higher ordering frequency and ARPU than casual/new users
Returning and power users on Android in primary metros represent the highest-value cohort
UPI is the dominant payment method among high-frequency orderers — premium checkout reliability is a key value proposition
Reviews from power/returning users mention specific restaurant loyalty and delivery speed expectations
Support tickets from high-value users focus on payment reliability and order accuracy — premium SLA opportunities
Platform distribution shows Android dominance — mobile-first membership experience is essential
City concentration: Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi NCR account for the majority of high-value orders

data_model.schema_notes: MECE design: city, platform, and user_type live in the users table only. Other tables reference users via FK (user_id). To analyze orders by city or platform, JOIN orders to users.

analyst.persona: You are Priya, a pragmatic senior data analyst. You analyze customer segments, ordering patterns, revenue cohorts, payment preferences, and business metrics to inform product strategy.

ux_researcher.persona: You are Kavya, a UX researcher focused on customer satisfaction, review themes, support pain points, and feature opportunity gaps that inform product decisions.

engineering_lead.persona: You are Rohan, an engineering lead who provides platform reliability data, service health metrics, and infrastructure context to inform product SLA commitments and technical feasibility.

analyst.description: Customer segments, ordering patterns, revenue analysis, payment preferences. JOIN orders to users for city/platform/user_type dimensions.

ux_researcher.description: Customer reviews, support tickets, usability research, and product change history. JOIN reviews/tickets to users for segment context.

engineering_lead.description: Service reliability data, deployment history, detailed error log — informs SLA promises for premium tier.

expected_key_findings: Power users order 4-5x more frequently than casual users and have significantly higher ARPU
Returning and power users together account for the majority of revenue
Android is the dominant platform among high-value users — mobile-first experience is critical
Bengaluru, Mumbai, and Delhi NCR concentrate the highest-value customer base
UPI is the preferred payment method for frequent orderers — payment reliability is a key satisfaction driver
Reviews from power users emphasize delivery speed, restaurant quality, and order accuracy
Support tickets from high-value users focus on payment issues and delivery delays — premium SLA opportunities
Service reliability data shows payment latency spikes affected user trust — premium tier should guarantee reliability
There is a natural segmentation between new/casual users (price-sensitive) and power/returning users (quality-sensitive)

levels.excellent: Clearly defines target segments using data-backed criteria (ordering frequency, ARPU, platform, city), with specific cohort sizes and behavioral patterns
levels.good: Identifies high-value segments but may lack precision in cohort definition or size estimates
levels.adequate: Recognizes that different user types exist but doesn't quantify or prioritize them

levels.excellent: Proposes features directly tied to identified pain points and satisfaction drivers (e.g., priority checkout, delivery guarantees, exclusive restaurants) with evidence from reviews and support data
levels.good: Proposes reasonable features connected to some user data
levels.adequate: Lists generic membership features without clear data linkage
levels.poor: Proposes features with no connection to customer analysis

levels.excellent: Pricing model backed by ARPU analysis, ordering frequency, and willingness-to-pay signals from the data — includes value proposition math
levels.good: Reasonable pricing with some data support
levels.adequate: Suggests a price point without strong justification
levels.poor: No pricing recommendation or completely arbitrary

levels.excellent: Systematically queries multiple agents, builds analysis from segmentation through to recommendation, uses both quantitative and qualitative evidence
levels.good: Uses multiple agents and builds a coherent analysis
levels.adequate: Explores some data but misses key dimensions or agents
levels.poor: Shallow analysis relying on a single agent or minimal data
